heroOverview: Macrostomia (ICD-10: Q18.4) is a rare congenital malformation in which the oral commissure — the corner where the upper and lower lips meet — extends further laterally than normal, producing an abnormally wide mouth opening. The condition arises from incomplete fusion of the maxillary and mandibular facial processes during embryonic development, typically between weeks 4 and 7 of gestation. Reported incidence is approximately 1 in 80,000 live births, though many cases go uncatalogued in registries. Macrostomia may be unilateral or bilateral, ranges in severity from a subtle notch to a significant cleft-like extension toward the ear, and frequently co-occurs with other first and second branchial arch anomalies such as preauricular tags, microtia, and mandibulofacial dysostosis. Treatment is surgical commissuroplasty, typically performed in infancy, and yields excellent functional and cosmetic results in experienced centers.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 80,000 live births; exact figures vary due to underreporting (literature estimates range 1:50,000–1:100,000)
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally; isolated macrostomia has no sex predominance
keyStats.avgAge: Diagnosed at birth or on prenatal ultrasound; surgical repair typically performed at 3–6 months
keyStats.globalCases: Rare; total global case series in the literature number in the hundreds

definition: Macrostomia (ICD-10: Q18.4) is a congenital enlargement of the oral fissure caused by failure of the embryonic facial processes to fuse fully at the angle of the mouth. During normal facial morphogenesis, the maxillary prominence and the mandibular prominence merge bilaterally at the commissure; incomplete fusion leaves a lateral cleft of varying depth. The condition is classified under congenital malformations of face and neck (ICD-10 block Q10–Q18). It may present as an isolated defect or as part of a syndrome, most notably Treacher Collins (mandibulofacial dysostosis), Goldenhar syndrome (oculoauriculovertebral spectrum), Nager syndrome, and Miller syndrome. In syndromic cases, associated features frequently include preauricular tags, microtia, malar hypoplasia, coloboma, vertebral anomalies, and cardiac defects. Severity is graded clinically: grade I is a small notch, grade II reaches the masseter muscle, and grade III extends to the tragus of the ear. Functional sequelae include drooling, feeding difficulty in infancy, articulation problems, and psychosocial impact from altered facial appearance. The condition is managed collaboratively by craniofacial surgeons, plastic surgeons, speech-language pathologists, and geneticists when a syndrome is suspected.

typesClassification.description: A unilateral or bilateral widening of the oral commissure occurring as a single defect without associated anomalies; represents roughly 50% of cases and responds well to commissuroplasty alone.
Extension of only one commissure; may be associated with ipsilateral ear anomalies and preauricular tags in the setting of hemifacial microsomia or Goldenhar syndrome.
Both commissures are widened, giving a characteristic appearance; more often associated with chromosomal or syndromic diagnoses.

Macrostomia occurring as one feature of a broader syndrome such as mandibulofacial dysostosis, oculoauriculovertebral spectrum, or a chromosome deletion; requires comprehensive multidisciplinary assessment.

primarySymptoms: A visibly wider mouth opening than expected for age, often apparent at birth or on newborn examination.
A lateral notch or cleft extending from the oral commissure toward the cheek or ear, ranging from millimetres to several centimetres.
Drooling caused by the inability of the lips to seal completely at the corners during swallowing and speech.
Feeding difficulties in infancy, including poor latch during breastfeeding and spillage of milk from the corners of the mouth.
Speech articulation difficulties, particularly with bilabial sounds (p, b, m) that require complete lip closure.
Asymmetry of the lower face when macrostomia is unilateral, which may be the presenting complaint at well-child visits.
Dry, irritated, or excoriated skin at the commissure due to constant moisture from saliva.
Psychosocial distress in older children related to facial appearance and social teasing.
Difficulty wearing a sealed oxygen mask, snorkel, or musical mouthpiece instrument if the defect is significant.
In syndromic cases, associated findings such as ear tags, hearing loss, malar flattening, or downward-slanting palpebral fissures.
earlyWarningSigns: A notch visible at the corner of the mouth on a newborn examination that extends beyond the normal lip junction.
Infant consistently losing milk from one or both corners during feeding despite good positioning.
Preauricular skin tags or a pit on the same side as the wider commissure, suggesting a branchial arch syndrome.
Prenatal ultrasound finding of a lateral facial cleft or widened oral fissure flagged by the sonographer.
emergencySigns: Severe feeding failure in a neonate leading to weight loss greater than 10% of birth weight — requires urgent feeding assessment and possible nasogastric supplementation.
Signs of an associated cardiac defect such as cyanosis, poor perfusion, or heart murmur in a syndromic infant — prompt cardiology evaluation.
Airway compromise in a neonate with concomitant mandibular hypoplasia and micrognathia causing posterior tongue displacement.
Wound dehiscence or infection following commissuroplasty with fever, erythema, and purulent discharge.

causes.description: Between gestational weeks 4 and 7, the maxillary and mandibular prominences normally fuse laterally to form the commissure. Failure or arrest of this fusion at any point along the fusion line produces a commissural cleft whose extent corresponds to the gestational week of the insult.

Trisomies, deletions, and duplications — particularly involving chromosomes 4p, 18, and 22q11 — disrupt expression of the transcription factors (including MSX1, PAX7, and TWIST1) that govern facial process fusion, producing macrostomia among other craniofacial anomalies.

Pathogenic variants in TCOF1 (Treacher Collins syndrome), EYA1 or SIX1 (branchio-oto-renal syndrome), and other genes controlling neural crest cell migration and first and second branchial arch development reduce the tissue mass available for complete commissural fusion.

Early placental insufficiency or amniotic band compression can restrict blood flow to facial prominences, preventing normal cellular proliferation and fusion; this mechanism is hypothesised for some sporadic unilateral cases.

Maternal exposure to retinoic acid, anticonvulsants, or alcohol during the critical window of facial development has been associated with branchial arch abnormalities including commissural widening, though macrostomia itself is rarely the sole teratogenic outcome.

A substantial proportion of isolated macrostomia cases arise with no identifiable chromosomal, genetic, or environmental cause; these appear to represent de novo developmental errors in facial process fusion.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of branchial arch anomalies
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives with macrotia, preauricular tags, microtia, or oculoauriculovertebral spectrum carry elevated risk for the same spectrum of defects through shared genetic determinants of branchial arch development.
Prenatal diagnosis of chromosome 22q11 deletion, trisomy 18, or 4p deletion significantly raises the probability of associated craniofacial malformations including widened oral commissure.

First-trimester alcohol use, retinoic acid derivatives, and valproate have been linked to branchial arch disruption syndromes; eliminating these exposures in pregnancy reduces the broader risk of congenital facial defects.
Autosomal recessive syndromes that include macrostomia (such as some forms of mandibulofacial dysostosis) occur at higher frequency in offspring of consanguineous parents.
Rupture of the amnion creates fibrous bands that can compress or amputate facial structures in utero; lateral facial clefts including commissural widening are a recognised consequence.

De novo mutations in developmental genes accumulate with paternal age; while the absolute effect on rare craniofacial conditions is small, advanced paternal age is an established risk factor for de novo pathogenic variants.

affectedDemographics: Neonates and infants of any sex, typically identified at birth
Children with known chromosomal syndromes, particularly 22q11 deletion or trisomy 18
Infants with other branchial arch anomalies, including microtia, preauricular tags, or mandibular hypoplasia
Children born to mothers with first-trimester retinoic acid or anticonvulsant exposure
Families with an autosomal dominant craniofacial syndrome such as Treacher Collins
diagnosisOverview: Macrostomia is diagnosed clinically by direct inspection of the oral commissure. In many cases, the diagnosis is made in the delivery room or at the newborn examination. When a lateral cleft or notch extends beyond the normal vermilion border at the commissure, the width of the oral fissure is compared against age-standardised norms — the interlabial distance at rest normally ranges from about 35 to 45 mm in term neonates. Grading (I–III) quantifies the extent of the cleft from the commissure toward the tragus. The key diagnostic step beyond clinical measurement is to determine whether macrostomia is isolated or part of a syndrome, which drives the scope of further investigation. All affected children should undergo a thorough dysmorphology assessment, including inspection for ear anomalies, malar hypoplasia, downward-slanting fissures, vertebral and limb findings, and cardiac auscultation. Genetic evaluation with chromosomal microarray is recommended when additional features are present. Hearing assessment is essential because branchial arch syndromes frequently affect ossicle development. Echocardiography is performed if cardiac anomalies are suspected clinically. Prenatal diagnosis may occur on detailed morphology ultrasound at 20 weeks or by fetal MRI when the ultrasound is ambiguous. Genetic counselling is offered to the family once a syndromic diagnosis is established.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the width of the oral fissure against normative standards and grades severity to guide surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician uses calipers or a ruler to measure the distance between the commissures at rest and at maximal mouth opening; it takes a minute or two and is entirely painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects submicroscopic deletions and duplications — particularly 22q11.2 deletions — that underlie syndromic macrostomia and have management implications beyond surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small blood draw from the infant; results typically take 2–4 weeks and will guide genetics counselling and surveillance for associated problems.

diagnosticTests.test: Brainstem auditory evoked response (BAER) / newborn hearing screen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects sensorineural or conductive hearing loss associated with branchial arch anomalies, which affects speech development planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Electrodes are placed on the scalp while the baby sleeps; the test lasts 30–60 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies structural cardiac defects in syndromic cases, particularly important before general anaesthesia for commissuroplasty.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ultrasound probe is placed on the chest; the test takes 20–40 minutes and is non-invasive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps bone and soft-tissue architecture before surgical repair, especially for more severe or bilateral cases or when mandibular hypoplasia coexists.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT takes a few minutes; MRI takes 30–60 minutes and may require sedation in infants.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates the functional impact of macrostomia on oral feeding, lip seal, and suckling mechanics in neonates and infants.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The therapist observes a full feed and may video-record swallowing; it is non-invasive and takes 30–60 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Surgical commissuroplasty is the definitive treatment for macrostomia and is recommended for all but the most trivial grade I cases. The procedure reconstructs the commissure by repairing the orbicularis oris muscle continuity and creating a normal-appearing lip angle, restoring both function and aesthetics. Most craniofacial centres perform repair between 3 and 6 months of age, when anaesthetic risk has decreased and before compensatory speech patterns become established. Several commissuroplasty techniques have been described, including straight-line, Z-plasty, and W-plasty repairs; each aims to minimise scar contracture and step deformity at the reconstructed commissure. Functional results — lip seal, feeding, drooling — typically improve dramatically within weeks of surgery. Cosmetic outcomes are highly satisfactory in experienced centres, with most patients requiring no revision. In syndromic cases, commissuroplasty is coordinated with repair of other anomalies and may be staged. Pre- and postoperative speech therapy supports articulation development, particularly bilabial sounds. Hearing aids or myringotomy tubes are arranged concurrently if conductive hearing loss is detected. Psychosocial support and liaison with early intervention services are important in families dealing with a syndrome diagnosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive feeding management (pre-surgery)
medicalTreatments.description: Specialised bottle teats, positioning strategies, and thickened feeds help neonates with macrostomia achieve adequate oral intake before surgical repair. A paediatric dietitian tracks growth and adjusts supplementation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves adequate nutrition in approximately 80–90% of mild-to-moderate cases, avoiding nasogastric tube insertion until surgery.

medicalTreatments.name: Speech and language therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Postoperative therapy targets bilabial consonants, oral motor coordination, and drool management, beginning shortly after healing is complete, typically at 3–6 months post-repair.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Most children with isolated macrostomia achieve age-appropriate articulation by school entry, with therapy accelerating progress by an estimated 30–40% compared with no intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Genetic counselling
medicalTreatments.description: For syndromic cases, clinical geneticists interpret chromosomal microarray and targeted gene panel results, advise on recurrence risk (up to 50% for autosomal dominant syndromes), and coordinate surveillance for associated organ anomalies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents diagnostic delays and allows early surveillance for cardiac, renal, vertebral, and hearing complications associated with specific syndromes.

medicalTreatments.name: Hearing rehabilitation (hearing aids or tubes)
medicalTreatments.description: Conductive hearing loss from ossicular or Eustachian tube anomalies, common in branchial arch syndromes, is treated with bone-anchored hearing aids or pressure-equalisation tubes to support speech development.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Early amplification within the first 6 months reduces speech-language delays by approximately 50% in children with moderate conductive hearing loss.

surgicalOptions.name: Commissuroplasty (straight-line repair)
surgicalOptions.description: Excision of the commissural cleft with direct layered closure reconstructing the orbicularis oris ring and creating a natural commissure angle; the simplest technique and preferred for mild to moderate cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Excellent functional and aesthetic results in over 90% of patients in published case series; revision required in fewer than 10% of cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Commissuroplasty with Z-plasty or W-plasty
surgicalOptions.description: Geometric skin rearrangement at the commissure that interrupts the straight-line scar and reduces the risk of notching or contracture at the repaired angle; preferred for more extensive defects or bilateral cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Functional success greater than 90%; minimises visible scar and step deformity compared with straight-line repair in grades II–III defects.

alternativeTreatments.name: Lip taping (pre-surgical)
alternativeTreatments.description: In some centres, adhesive taping to approximate the commissure in the first weeks of life may reduce the extent of surgical repair needed, though evidence is limited to small case series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Oral motor exercises
alternativeTreatments.description: Targeted lip and perioral strengthening exercises, guided by a speech-language pathologist, can partially compensate for mild lip seal deficits before and after surgery.

whySeeSpecialist: A craniofacial plastic surgeon or paediatric plastic surgeon with experience in perioral reconstruction should lead macrostomia management, because commissuroplasty technique directly determines long-term cosmetic and functional outcomes. A geneticist or clinical dysmorphologist should be involved whenever additional anomalies are present, given the broad range of syndromes that include macrostomia. Audiologists and speech-language pathologists are integral team members from birth. For bilateral or syndromic cases, referral to a dedicated craniofacial centre with multidisciplinary team meetings is strongly recommended.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a paediatric craniofacial or plastic surgeon who performs commissuroplasty regularly — ideally at least several cases per year — and who is part of a multidisciplinary craniofacial team. Ask specifically about their technique for minimising scar and step deformity. For syndromic diagnosis, look for a clinical geneticist familiar with branchial arch malformation syndromes. Access to audiological services and a paediatric speech-language pathologist within the same centre simplifies coordinated care.
preventionStrategies: Genetic counselling and carrier testing before pregnancy for families with a known autosomal dominant craniofacial syndrome.
Prenatal chromosomal microarray or targeted gene panel testing when a family history or maternal carrier status is established.
Avoidance of teratogenic medications (isotretinoin, valproate) during the first trimester, substituting safer alternatives under medical supervision.
Folic acid supplementation (400–800 mcg daily) from preconception through the first trimester, which supports general neural crest cell development.
Detailed mid-trimester morphology ultrasound with fetal facial views to facilitate early diagnosis and prenatal counselling.
lifestyleModifications: Use wide-based or specialist orthodontic bottle teats before repair to minimise spillage and support adequate nutrition.
Apply a gentle barrier cream (petroleum jelly) to the commissure area to protect against salivary maceration of skin.
Encourage lip closure exercises and oral play appropriate to developmental age in the post-surgical period.
Attend all scheduled craniofacial team follow-up appointments, particularly the 3- and 12-month post-surgical reviews.
Engage with peer support organisations for families of children with facial differences to address psychosocial impact.

recommended: High-calorie, age-appropriate feeds (breast milk or formula with caloric fortifier if weight gain is poor before surgery)
Thickened feeds if liquid spillage from the commissure is causing aspiration risk
Puréed and soft foods introduced at developmental milestones after surgical repair
Adequate protein and micronutrient intake to support wound healing in the post-surgical period
avoid: Hard, crunchy, or sharp-edged foods for 4–6 weeks after commissuroplasty to protect the repair
Excessive fluid volumes given quickly, which stress a wide oral fissure and cause spillage
Acidic or spicy foods immediately post-surgery that may irritate the healing commissure

exerciseGuidelines: No specific exercise restriction applies for macrostomia itself. Following commissuroplasty, infants should avoid activities that stretch the mouth widely — such as prolonged crying without consolation efforts — for 4 to 6 weeks. Guided perioral stretching exercises, prescribed by the speech-language pathologist after healing, improve commissure mobility and speech outcomes. As children grow, full physical activity is encouraged.
dailyManagement: Before surgery: ensure adequate nutrition by tracking weight weekly and reporting any drop below the 3rd centile to the medical team.
Keep the commissure dry and clean to prevent skin breakdown; gentle cleaning with saline after feeds.
After surgery: follow wound care instructions from the surgical team, typically gentle cleansing with dilute antiseptic twice daily.
Attend all speech therapy sessions; practice target sounds (b, p, m) at home as instructed.
Communicate openly with teachers and carers about the child's condition to support social inclusion at school.
prognosis: The long-term outlook for children with isolated macrostomia is excellent. Commissuroplasty reliably restores lip seal, eliminates drooling, and produces a cosmetically acceptable commissure in over 90% of patients. Speech development proceeds normally in the great majority after surgical repair and speech therapy. Social and psychological outcomes are significantly better when surgery is performed early and the child receives appropriate developmental support. For syndromic macrostomia, the prognosis depends on the severity of associated anomalies — particularly cardiac defects, hearing loss, and cognitive development — rather than on the oral commissure defect itself. Regular craniofacial team review through childhood and adolescence ensures timely management of any residual or developing issues.
recoveryTimeline: Soft tissue swelling at the repair site typically peaks at 24–48 hours post-surgery and resolves within 1–2 weeks. Feeding via a specialised teat or cup returns within 48 hours in most infants. The surgical wound is fully healed by 4–6 weeks. Scar maturation and final cosmetic assessment are performed at 6–12 months. Speech progress is reviewed at 12 months post-repair. Revision surgery, if needed for step deformity or width discrepancy, is generally deferred until after 18 months of age.
complications: Wound dehiscence — partial reopening of the commissure repair, occurring in fewer than 10% of cases and usually managed by secondary closure.
Scar hypertrophy or notching at the repaired commissure — the most common long-term cosmetic concern, addressed by revision surgery or laser treatment.
Persistent mild widening — if complete obliteration of the cleft is not achieved at primary repair, a second-stage procedure may be needed.
Feeding difficulties persisting after surgery — rare, but may require extended speech therapy or dietetic support.
Under-correction or asymmetry — technical complication requiring revision commissuroplasty, performed after 18 months when tissues are more robust.
General anaesthesia risks — standard paediatric anaesthetic considerations, greater in infants under 3 months or those with associated cardiac or airway anomalies.

supportResources.name: Cleft Lip and Palate Association (CLAPA)
supportResources.description: UK-based organisation supporting children and families with all congenital oral and facial cleft conditions, including commissural clefts.

supportResources.name: Children's Craniofacial Association
supportResources.description: US nonprofit providing educational resources, peer support, and camp programmes for children with craniofacial conditions including macrostomia.

supportResources.name: FACES: The National Craniofacial Association
supportResources.description: Provides financial assistance, educational materials, and peer networking for children and adults with craniofacial anomalies.

sources.title: Gosain AK, Recinos RF. A novel approach to correction of the soft tissue component of the cleft lip commissuroplasty. Plast Reconstr Surg, 2003
Monasterio FO et al. Commissuroplasty in macrostomia. Ann Plast Surg, 1997
Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man (OMIM) — Macrostomia (entry 164200)

National Organization for Rare Disorders (NORD) — Oculoauriculovertebral Spectrum

confusedWithConditions.name: Cleft lip and palate
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cleft lip involves a vertical split through the lip and alveolus, whereas macrostomia is a horizontal extension of the oral commissure; the anatomical axis of the defect is different.

confusedWithConditions.name: Branchial cleft sinus or cyst
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Branchial cleft cysts are lateral neck masses, not oral commissure defects; they share the same developmental origin (branchial arches) but have completely different anatomy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Other congenital malformations of lips
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: General lip malformations may involve vermilion borders, lip pits, or midline defects, whereas macrostomia specifically involves horizontal widening at the commissure.

relatedConditions.name: Microstomia
relatedConditions.relevance: The converse condition — an abnormally small mouth opening — arising from the same embryological process of commissural fusion, often managed by the same surgical team.

relatedConditions.name: Macroglossia
relatedConditions.relevance: Enlargement of the tongue can co-occur with macrostomia in certain syndromes; both affect oral function and feeding in infancy.

relatedConditions.name: Other branchial cleft malformations
relatedConditions.relevance: Macrostomia shares its developmental origin with branchial arch anomalies, and multiple defects frequently co-occur in the same patient.

relatedConditions.name: Cleft lip and palate
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common orofacial cleft condition, managed by the same craniofacial teams who treat macrostomia and sharing some surgical principles.

faqs.question: What is macrostomia?
faqs.answer: Macrostomia is a congenital condition in which the corner of the mouth extends further toward the cheek than normal, creating an abnormally wide oral opening. It results from incomplete fusion of the facial processes during embryonic development.

faqs.question: How common is macrostomia?
faqs.answer: Macrostomia affects approximately 1 in 80,000 live births, making it a rare condition. Exact figures vary because mild cases are often underreported in birth defect registries.

faqs.question: Is macrostomia the same as cleft lip?
faqs.answer: No. Cleft lip is a vertical split through the lip and often the palate, while macrostomia is a horizontal widening of the oral commissure (corner of the mouth) toward the cheek. They involve different anatomical structures and are repaired with different surgical techniques.

faqs.question: What causes macrostomia?
faqs.answer: Macrostomia results from incomplete fusion of the maxillary and mandibular facial processes during weeks 4–7 of fetal development. It can be isolated or part of a genetic syndrome such as Treacher Collins or Goldenhar syndrome.

faqs.question: When is surgery for macrostomia performed?
faqs.answer: Commissuroplasty is typically performed between 3 and 6 months of age, after anaesthetic risk has fallen and before compensatory speech patterns form. The timing is planned with a craniofacial team.

faqs.question: What does commissuroplasty involve?
faqs.answer: Commissuroplasty closes the lateral commissural cleft by repairing the orbicularis oris muscle and recreating a natural lip angle. Techniques include straight-line, Z-plasty, and W-plasty repair; all are performed under general anaesthesia and take 1–2 hours.

faqs.question: Will my child have a visible scar after commissuroplasty?
faqs.answer: Some degree of scar is inevitable, but experienced surgeons minimise it with careful muscle repair and geometric scar-breaking techniques. Scars typically fade significantly over 12–18 months and are usually not conspicuous at conversational distance.

faqs.question: Can macrostomia affect speech?
faqs.answer: Yes. An incomplete lip seal makes bilabial sounds (p, b, m) difficult and allows air to escape from the commissure. Most children achieve normal articulation after commissuroplasty and speech therapy, typically by school entry.

faqs.question: Does macrostomia affect feeding in babies?
faqs.answer: Yes. The widened commissure prevents a complete lip seal around the nipple or teat, causing milk spillage and sometimes prolonged feeding times. Specialist teats and positioning help before surgery; repair dramatically improves feeding in most infants.

faqs.question: Is macrostomia always associated with a syndrome?
faqs.answer: Roughly half of cases are isolated, with no other anomalies and no identifiable syndrome. The other half occur as part of branchial arch syndromes such as Goldenhar or Treacher Collins. A full dysmorphology assessment is performed to distinguish the two.

faqs.question: What tests are done after macrostomia is diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Evaluation includes a dysmorphology exam, chromosomal microarray if additional features are present, hearing assessment, and echocardiography if a cardiac defect is suspected. The extent of testing depends on whether the macrostomia appears isolated or syndromic.

faqs.question: Will my child need more than one surgery?
faqs.answer: Most children with isolated macrostomia require only one commissuroplasty. Revision surgery is performed in fewer than 10% of cases to address residual widening or scar notching. Syndromic cases may need additional procedures for other anomalies.

faqs.question: What is the long-term outcome for macrostomia?
faqs.answer: The outlook is excellent for isolated macrostomia. Over 90% of patients achieve a functionally and cosmetically satisfactory result from commissuroplasty. Speech and feeding normalise in the great majority, and psychological outcomes are good with early surgical repair and family support.

faqs.question: Can macrostomia be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Macrostomia can sometimes be seen on a detailed 20-week morphology ultrasound, particularly if the defect is significant or if other branchial arch anomalies are present. Fetal MRI provides more detail when ultrasound findings are uncertain.

faqs.question: Is macrostomia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Isolated macrostomia is usually sporadic with no clear hereditary pattern. When it occurs as part of an autosomal dominant syndrome such as Treacher Collins, the recurrence risk per pregnancy is 50%. Genetic counselling clarifies recurrence risk based on the specific diagnosis.

faqs.question: What specialist treats macrostomia?
faqs.answer: A paediatric craniofacial or plastic surgeon performs the commissuroplasty. Supporting care involves a clinical geneticist, audiologist, speech-language pathologist, and paediatric dietitian, ideally coordinated through a multidisciplinary craniofacial centre.

faqs.question: Does macrostomia cause any hearing problems?
faqs.answer: Macrostomia itself does not affect hearing, but when it occurs as part of a branchial arch syndrome, associated ossicular or middle-ear anomalies can cause conductive hearing loss. A newborn hearing screen and audiology follow-up are recommended for all children with macrostomia.

faqs.question: What skin care does the commissure area need before surgery?
faqs.answer: Before surgical repair, applying a gentle barrier cream such as petroleum jelly after feeds protects the skin around the commissure from salivary maceration. Gentle cleansing with saline or plain water and keeping the area dry reduces the risk of skin breakdown.

faqs.question: Can speech therapy replace surgery for macrostomia?
faqs.answer: Speech therapy alone cannot correct the structural defect. Surgery is needed to restore lip seal and commissure anatomy. Speech therapy complements surgery by helping the child develop correct articulation after the structural correction has been made.

faqs.question: How is macrostomia graded?
faqs.answer: Macrostomia is graded from I to III based on how far the commissural cleft extends: grade I is a small notch, grade II reaches the masseter muscle, and grade III extends toward the tragus of the ear. Grading guides the surgical technique and expected complexity.

metaDescription: Macrostomia is a congenital wide mouth opening caused by incomplete facial fusion. Learn about commissuroplasty surgery, symptoms, and outcomes. Doctor-reviewed.
